one_line_takeaway: A literature-grounded taxonomy and metric framework (UAR, PAR, PED, RRS, TTC, OORAR, CES) for reasoning about agentic-LLM attack surfaces across tools, RAG, and multi-agent autonomy -- but the metrics/harness are proposed definitions, not results the authors measured themselves.
key_finding: Synthesizing the reviewed literature (not the authors' own experiments), the paper reports several cross-study patterns: (1) LLM+tools implies RCE risk unless proven otherwise -- citing LLMSmith's finding of 19 RCE vulnerabilities across 11 agent frameworks with real exploits, later leading to CVE remediation [ref 16/23 in paper]; (2) indirect prompt injection is practical and hard to eliminate, per live end-to-end compromise demonstrations and systematic benchmarking (e.g., BIPIA) showing models fail to reliably separate retrieved content from instructions [refs 13, 26]; (3) RAG is not intrinsically safer -- citing PoisonedRAG's demonstration of high-success targeted responses via small corpus perturbations [ref 28/2], and a NAACL 2025 study ('RAG LLMs are Not Safer') finding retrieval can introduce new unsafe-output failure modes [ref 8]; (4) multi-agent orchestration amplifies risk, with early testbeds showing propagation of malicious messages across agents and only partial mitigation via 'vaccination'-style defenses at a task-performance cost [ref 20]. The SoK's own synthesized conclusion (not a cited third-party number) is that tool- and retrieval-level attacks are predominantly supported by grade A-B evidence, while defenses -- especially for multi-agent and lifecycle-level threats -- remain largely grade C, indicating an empirical maturity gap between attack demonstration and defense validation.
core_contribution: A systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) of the attack surface of agentic LLM systems (tool use, RAG, and autonomous/multi-agent loops). The paper's own artifacts are: (1) a reference pipeline/trust-boundary diagram with a Trusted Computing Base (TCB) definition (core model weights + system prompt, tool-execution substrate, secret storage, RAG indexing pipeline/store); (2) a taxonomy of 7 attacker goals (G1-G7: data exfiltration, integrity/safety bypass, privilege escalation, resource abuse/DoS-DoC, fraud, persistence/backdoor, supply-chain compromise), 7 attack vectors by pipeline stage, and 5 composite attack paths (P1 direct-prompt-to-tool-misuse, P2 indirect-content-to-LLM-to-tool, P3 cross-tool pivot, P4 index-poisoning-to-query-to-response, P5 multi-agent hop), mapped to OWASP LLM Top-10 2025 and MITRE ATLAS technique IDs (Table 2); (3) a causal threat graph formalism with attacker classes (external attacker, malicious content provider, supply-chain attacker, insider/developer, compromised API/service) and defined defense-interdict points; (4) a set of proposed attacker-aware metrics -- Unsafe Action Rate (UAR), Policy Adherence Rate (PAR), Privilege-Escalation Distance (PED, shortest path on the causal graph from untrusted input to a privileged action), Time-to-Contain (TTC), Patch Half-Life (PHL), Retrieval Risk Score (RRS), Out-of-Role Action Rate (OORAR), and Cost-Exploit Susceptibility (CES) -- with formal definitions and a conceptual (not implemented/run) evaluation harness design; and (5) a defense-in-depth framework and practitioner deployment/monitoring checklist (Appendices A/B, not examined in full text extracted here). The evidence base is a structured literature review (a 'PRISMA-lite' sweep) of ~100 candidate sources screened down to ~40 full-text reviewed, yielding a final corpus of 14 peer-reviewed papers (6 from 2024, 6 from 2025, 2 from 2023), 5 major preprints (2023-2025), 3 standards/industry documents (OWASP, NIST, MITRE ATLAS), and select industry blogs/artifacts, each graded A-D for evidence quality.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section 3) defines five adversary classes: (1) External Attacker (unprivileged, interacts via prompts or malicious links); (2) Malicious Content Provider (indirect influence by poisoning content the agent later consumes -- web pages, PDFs, API responses -- without being a direct agent user); (3) Supply-Chain Attacker (compromises plugins, dependencies, model checkpoints, or vector databases pre-deployment); (4) Insider/Developer (has direct access to config/prompts/secrets/corpora; mostly treated as unintentional vulnerability but can deliberately weaken safeguards); (5) Compromised API/Service (an attacker who takes over a trusted external service the agent relies on, e.g. email or a vector DB, and abuses the agent's trust in it). Assets at risk: sensitive data, credentials/API keys, underlying infrastructure/compute cost, and integrity of agent behavior/policy adherence, mapped onto CIA plus alignment/policy-integrity and auditability. Baseline assumed defenses: some sandboxing, some prompt filters, and a well-intentioned but not fully security-savvy developer -- explicitly not assuming any 'magical' ML fix. The LLM itself is treated as a black-box, untrusted code/action generator whose outputs must be validated before execution.
limitations: The paper is explicit that: the proposed metrics (UAR, PAR, PED, RRS, TTC, OORAR, CES, PHL) and the benchmarking harness are defined/proposed but not implemented or empirically run in this work -- no new measurements are reported for any of them. The literature corpus, while structured, is modest (~40 full-text-reviewed sources, 14 peer-reviewed core papers) and reflects a still-young research area (2023-2025). The authors themselves flag gaps: post-exploitation persistence and concealment in agentic systems are underexplored; fine-grained adversarial perturbations in retrieved text are rare in the literature; and reproducible, common testbeds for agent tool misuse are only beginning to appear. Coverage of closed/proprietary API-provider compromise is explicitly out of scope. As a synthesis paper, its conclusions inherit the biases and gaps of the underlying corpus (e.g., heavy reliance on a small number of highly-cited attack demonstrations such as PoisonedRAG and the LLMSmith RCE study for its strongest claims).

datasets_used: None (no experiments run); the paper's only 'dataset' is its own literature review corpus of surveyed papers/standards, not an experimental dataset
quantitative_results: Paper's own corpus-construction statistics (Section 5.1): ~100 initial candidate sources found via database/keyword search plus snowballing, screened down to ~40 for full-text review; final corpus = 14 peer-reviewed papers (6 from 2024, 6 from 2025, 2 from 2023) + 5 major preprints (2023-2025) + 3 standards/industry documents (OWASP, NIST, MITRE ATLAS) + select industry blogs/artifacts
Cited (not the SoK's own result): LLMSmith found 19 RCE vulnerabilities across 11 agent frameworks with successful real-world exploitation [attributed to reference 16/23 in the paper]

Cited (not the SoK's own result): PoisonedRAG demonstrated high-success targeted/attacker-chosen responses via small, controlled corpus perturbations against RAG systems [attributed to reference 28/2]
Cited (not the SoK's own result): a NAACL 2025 study found RAG-augmented LLMs are 'not safer' than non-RAG baselines on certain safety measures [attributed to reference 8]
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines -- the paper instead positions its scope against prior qualitative surveys: Deng et al.'s agent-threat survey, Narajala and Narayan's agent-focused threat model, and TRiSM-oriented governance reviews, arguing its own contribution is a causal threat graph tied to privileged effects plus a CI-suitable attacker-aware measurement harness that those prior works lack
